title: Superconductivity near quarter- and half-filling of a strongly correlated triangular Hubbard band in twisted trilayer WSe2
summary: The triangular-lattice Hubbard model is one of the paradigmatic settings for studying correlated electronic systems with geometric frustration that can give rise to a variety of phases. Recently, twisted bilayer TMD systems have emerged as attractive experimental platforms for realising such a model in the moderate-correlation regime. Alternating twisted trilayer TMD systems are expected to realise stronger correlations than bilayer systems owing to their mirror symmetry. Here we report the discovery of superconductivity near quarter- and half-filling, together with various correlated phases, in alternating twisted trilayer WSe2 (TTWSe2). At half filling, a robust correlated insulating state persists over a broad range of displacement fields, signalling the strongly correlated regime. Upon doping, superconductivity emerges over an extended region of the phase diagram. Near quarter filling, another superconducting state appears flanked by correlated metals. Our results establish the TTWSe2 system as a compelling platform for studying strongly correlated triangular-lattice Hubbard physics.

AI.tldr: This paper reports the discovery of superconductivity and various correlated phases in alternating twisted trilayer WSe2 (TTWSe2) systems, establishing it as a platform for studying strongly correlated triangular-lattice Hubbard physics.
AI.motivation: The triangular-lattice Hubbard model is a paradigmatic setting for studying correlated electronic systems with geometric frustration. Twisted bilayer TMD systems have emerged as experimental platforms, and alternating twisted trilayer TMD systems are expected to realize stronger correlations due to mirror symmetry.
AI.method: The authors studied alternating twisted trilayer WSe2 (TTWSe2) near quarter- and half-filling using transport measurements under displacement fields.
AI.result: At half-filling, a robust correlated insulating state persists over a broad range of displacement fields. Upon doping, superconductivity emerges over an extended region. Near quarter filling, another superconducting state appears flanked by correlated metals.
AI.conclusion: The TTWSe2 system is established as a compelling platform for studying strongly correlated triangular-lattice Hubbard physics, with potential for further exploration of correlated phases.

title: Unveiling the Interplay of Charge and Magnetic Excitations in HgBa$_2$Ca$_2$Cu$_3$O$_{8+δ}$
summary: Unraveling the mechanism that binds electrons into Cooper pairs in cuprate high-temperature superconductors remains one of the most fundamental challenges in condensed-matter physics. While both magnetic interactions and lattice vibrations are known to govern key electronic properties, their possible cooperation has never been directly observed. We investigate HgBa$_2$Ca$_2$Cu$_3$O$_{8+δ}$ (Hg1223) - the cuprate with the highest $T_{\mathrm{c}}$ at ambient pressure - as a magnifying glass to probe the possible entwining of the excitations at the core of the pairing. Using resonant inelastic X-ray scattering, we find that the charge response is dominated by dynamic charge density fluctuations (CDF) extending up to several hundred meV, where magnetic excitations reside. At the same momentum where CDF are most intense, the paramagnon energy exhibits a pronounced softening, revealing a strong interplay among charge, lattice, and spin excitations. Our results point to a cooperative mechanism in which dynamic charge fluctuations mediate the coupling between lattice, charge and spin degrees of freedom-shedding new light on the fundamental origin of high-$T_{\mathrm{c}}$ superconductivity.

AI.tldr: This study investigates the mechanism of Cooper pair formation in cuprate high-temperature superconductors, specifically HgBa2Ca2Cu3O8+δ (Hg1223), using resonant inelastic X-ray scattering. The research reveals dynamic charge density fluctuations (CDF) that interact with magnetic excitations (paramagnons), suggesting a cooperative mechanism where charge fluctuations mediate coupling between lattice, charge, and spin degrees of freedom.
AI.motivation: The fundamental challenge in condensed-matter physics is understanding how electrons form Cooper pairs in cuprate high-temperature superconductors. While magnetic interactions and lattice vibrations are known to be important, their cooperative relationship has never been directly observed. This study aims to uncover this cooperative mechanism.
AI.method: Resonant inelastic X-ray scattering (RIXS) was used to probe the Hg1223 cuprate compound - the highest Tc material at ambient pressure. This technique allows direct observation of charge, lattice, and spin excitations and their interactions.
AI.result: The charge response is dominated by dynamic charge density fluctuations (CDF) extending up to several hundred meV, where magnetic excitations reside. At the momentum where CDF are most intense, the paramagnon energy shows pronounced softening, revealing strong interplay among charge, lattice, and spin excitations.
AI.conclusion: The results point to a cooperative mechanism in which dynamic charge fluctuations mediate the coupling between lattice, charge, and spin degrees of freedom. This provides new insights into the fundamental origin of high-Tc superconductivity in cuprates.

title: Ambient and Pressure Dependent Superconductivity with Hydrogen Storage Potential in Quaternary Hydride LiMgZr2H12: A Comprehensive First-principles Insights
summary: Molecular hydrides have attracted relatively less attention in the search for high Tc superconductors because their hydrogen quasi-molecular units tend to be electronically inactive for superconductivity. In contrast, hydrogen rich compounds under high pressure have been widely considered strong candidates for achieving room-temperature superconductivity. However, their dependence on extreme pressure conditions significantly constrains their practical applicability. This work investigates hydrogen-rich superconducting materials that may be stable under ambient pressure conditions. Motivated by recent studies on the MgZrH2n family, a LiMgZr2H12 structure with Pmmm symmetry was designed. The mechanical, thermodynamic, and dynamical stability of the compound, together with its electronic and optical properties, were systematically investigated using first-principles calculations. Li doping in LiMgZr2H12 significantly increases the hydrogen derived contribution near the Fermi level (EF) and strengthens the electron-phonon coupling constant (λ) compared with MgZrH6. LiMgZr2H12 exhibits a critical temperature of 72.76 K at ambient pressure, which is further enhanced by applying pressure. At 10 GPa the critical temperature increases to 77.3 K. Elastic property analysis shows that the material remains mechanically stable over the pressure range studied (0-10 GPa). It also behaves like a ductile material suitable for current carrying applications. The material has a high machinability index, which is much higher than that of stainless steels. In addition, LiMgZr2H12 exhibits a gravimetric hydrogen storage capacity of 5.36 wt%, indicating its potential as a promising candidate for hybrid hydrogen storage technologies. This work offers a new direction for designing high-Tc hydrides at ambient conditions.

AI.tldr: This work investigates LiMgZr2H12 as a hydrogen-rich superconductor stable at ambient pressure, achieving Tc of 72.76 K (77.3 K at 10 GPa) through first-principles calculations, with potential applications in superconductivity and hydrogen storage.
AI.motivation: Molecular hydrides have been overlooked for high-Tc superconductors due to inactive hydrogen units, while hydrogen-rich compounds under high pressure face practical constraints. This work seeks hydrogen-rich superconductors stable at ambient pressure, motivated by MgZrH2n family studies.
AI.method: First-principles calculations were used to investigate mechanical, thermodynamic, and dynamical stability, along with electronic and optical properties of LiMgZr2H12 with Pmmm symmetry.
AI.result: Li doping increases hydrogen-derived contribution near EF and strengthens electron-phonon coupling (λ) vs MgZrH6. Tc = 72.76 K at ambient pressure, 77.3 K at 10 GPa. Material is mechanically stable (0-10 GPa), ductile, with high machinability (higher than stainless steels) and 5.36 wt% hydrogen storage capacity.
AI.conclusion: LiMgZr2H12 is a promising candidate for high-Tc hydrides at ambient conditions, offering new direction for designing practical superconductors and hybrid hydrogen storage technologies.

title: Mathematical Basis for Analyzing Superconducting Phase Transitions Using Catastrophe Theory
summary: We establish a rigorous mathematical bridge from quantum many-body path integrals to the cusp catastrophe model by Lyapunov-Schmidt reduction, which provides a theoretical foundation for analyzing superconducting phase transition using the catastrophe theory. First, it is proved that, near the critical point the infinite-dimensional effective action is diffeomorphic to a finite-dimensional catastrophe. Secondly, starting from Ginzburg-Landau free energy functional, the Euler-Lagrange partial differential equation can be reduced to the cusp catastrophe model. Thirdly, the fermionic imaginary-time path integral to the cusp catastrophe is derived through the Hubbard-Stratonovich transformation, Matsubara frequency expansion, and Grassmann algebra. Furthermore, we connect this framework with the adsorption potential theory we proposed, elucidating the catastrophic topological nature of the electron pairing mechanism in high-temperature superconductivity. The precise microscopic derivation of the adsorption potential from first-principles electronic structure calculations would strengthen the predictive power of the theory.

title: Exact Dynamics of Topological Order Across a CDW--SPT Transition
summary: We investigate the nonequilibrium dynamics of a one-dimensional interacting system across a transition from a charge-density-wave (CDW) phase to a symmetry-protected topological (SPT) phase. Starting from a CDW initial state, we study both sudden quenches and slow ramps into the SPT regime. While the CDW order melts under both protocols, the fate of topological order is sharply different. Following a sudden quench, long-range SPT order does not emerge because the post-quench state contains a finite density of excitations above the topological ground state. In contrast, slow ramps allow the system to follow the instantaneous ground state away from the critical region, enabling the buildup of SPT order with deviations governed by Kibble-Zurek defect production. The dynamics is solvable via a unitary mapping to a quadratic fermionic Hamiltonian, allowing us to compute the Loschmidt echo, correlation functions, and string correlator. The Loschmidt rate function exhibits cusps signaling dynamical quantum phase transitions, while the correlation dynamics reveal the contrasting mechanisms governing quenches and ramps across the transition. These results demonstrate that entering the topological regime is not sufficient for the emergence of topological order; the decisive factor is the suppression of excitation production during the evolution.

AI.tldr: This paper studies nonequilibrium dynamics of a 1D interacting system crossing from a charge-density-wave (CDW) phase to a symmetry-protected topological (SPT) phase. Using both sudden quenches and slow ramps as initialization protocols, the authors find that while CDW order melts in both cases, only slow ramps allow the emergence of SPT order due to reduced excitation production. The dynamics is solved via mapping to quadratic fermions, enabling calculations of Loschmidt echo and correlation functions.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to understand how topological order emerges during nonequilibrium dynamics across a phase transition, specifically whether simply entering the topological regime is sufficient for SPT order to develop.
AI.method: The study employs unitary mapping to a quadratic fermionic Hamiltonian, allowing exact computation of Loschmidt echo, correlation functions, and string correlator. Both sudden quench and slow ramp protocols are compared.
AI.result: Sudden quenches produce finite excitation density above the topological ground state, preventing long-range SPT order. Slow ramps allow the system to follow the instantaneous ground state, enabling SPT order buildup with Kibble-Zurek defect production. Cusps in the Loschmidt rate function signal dynamical quantum phase transitions.
AI.conclusion: Entering the topological regime is not sufficient for topological order emergence; the decisive factor is suppressing excitation production during evolution. This highlights the importance of nonequilibrium preparation protocols in generating SPT phases.

title: Reconfigurable Strain Gradient Polarity in Crystalline Oxide Nanomembranes for Controlled Bending of Functional Materials
summary: We report the fabrication and mechanical characterization of a "bubble" geometry for accessing local strain gradients using freestanding, single-crystalline manganite nanomembranes: a switchable bistable nanodrum, with opposite strain gradient polarities. By leveraging epitaxial strain as a source of pre-strain and with control of geometrical and mechanical boundary conditions, the fabricated device can support strain gradients with strain variation ranging from 0.01% to 1%. Switching energetics can be designed to configure the bubble morphology. By providing a mechanical framework for sustained strain gradients, this platform supports scalable oxide membrane applications such as the mechanical manipulation of magnetism, coupled to local probes.

AI.tldr: This paper reports the fabrication and mechanical characterization of a "bubble" geometry in freestanding, single-crystalline manganite nanomembranes that can support switchable bistable strain gradients with opposite polarities. The device leverages epitaxial strain as pre-strain with controlled geometrical and mechanical boundary conditions, achieving strain variation from 0.01% to 1%.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to develop a platform for accessing and controlling local strain gradients in oxide membranes, which is essential for scalable applications such as mechanical manipulation of magnetism coupled to local probes.
AI.method: The method involves fabricating "bubble" geometry nanomembranes from single-crystalline manganites, using epitaxial strain as a source of pre-strain, and controlling geometrical and mechanical boundary conditions to create switchable bistable nanodrums with configurable switching energetics.
AI.result: The device successfully supports strain gradients with variation ranging from 0.01% to 1%, with opposite strain gradient polarities in a switchable bistable configuration. The switching energetics can be designed to configure the bubble morphology.
AI.conclusion: This platform provides a mechanical framework for sustained strain gradients and enables scalable oxide membrane applications, particularly for mechanical manipulation of magnetism with potential for coupling to local probes.

title: Proximity-induced unconventional superconductivity and chiral topological phases in twisted graphene/NbSe$_2$ van der Waals heterostructure
summary: We study proximity-induced unconventional superconductivity in a twisted graphene/NbSe$_2$ van der Waals heterostructure using the Bogoliubov-de Gennes formalism. The normal-state parameters of proximitized graphene are extracted from ab initio calculations at a twist angle of $23.4^\circ$, which reduces the common symmetry of the heterostructure to $\mathbf{C}_3$. We construct symmetry-allowed superconducting gap functions of the graphene layer according to the irreducible representations of the $\mathbf{C}_3$ group, containing singlet and triplet pairing channels and their mixtures. Computing the topological invariants as a function of the mixing parameters, we find a rich phase diagram of chiral topological superconducting phases, characterized by nonzero Chern numbers $C\in\{-4,-2,2,4\}$. While the nature of the superconducting order parameter of NbSe$_2$ remains debated, the formation of the van der Waals heterostructure and the related symmetry reduction can alter the relative stability of competing pairing channels, potentially stabilizing a chiral component that is proximity-induced into graphene and triggers the topological phases identified here, making the twisted graphene/NbSe$_2$ heterostructure a promising platform for chiral topological superconductivity detectable via quasiparticle interference imaging and transport measurements.

AI.tldr: This study investigates proximity-induced unconventional superconductivity in a twisted graphene/NbSe2 van der Waals heterostructure using Bogoliubov-de Gennes formalism, revealing rich phase diagrams of chiral topological superconducting phases with Chern numbers C∈{-4,-2,2,4}.
AI.motivation: The study aims to explore and stabilize chiral topological superconductivity in twisted graphene/NbSe2 heterostructures, as the formation of the van der Waals heterostructure and related symmetry reduction can alter the relative stability of competing pairing channels and potentially induce chiral components into graphene.
AI.method: The researchers employ the Bogoliubov-de Gennes formalism, extract normal-state parameters of proximitized graphene from ab initio calculations at a twist angle of 23.4°, construct symmetry-allowed superconducting gap functions according to irreducible representations of the C3 group (containing singlet and triplet pairing channels and their mixtures), and compute topological invariants as a function of mixing parameters.
AI.result: The study finds a rich phase diagram of chiral topological superconducting phases characterized by nonzero Chern numbers C∈{-4,-2,2,4}, indicating the twisted graphene/NbSe2 heterostructure as a promising platform for chiral topological superconductivity.
AI.conclusion: The twisted graphene/NbSe2 heterostructure is a promising platform for chiral topological superconductivity detectable via quasiparticle interference imaging and transport measurements.

title: Invariants of Sequential Circuits and Generalized Non-Abelian Statistics
summary: Non-invertible symmetries in quantum many-body systems generally give rise to sequential unitary circuits that move symmetry defects. In this paper, we investigate invariants defined by sequences of such circuits, which move non-invertible defects and generate a Berry phase evaluated on quantum states with defects. We show that this Berry phase generally defines an invariant under local deformations, provided that the sequential circuits preserve the locality of those deformations. This invariant also rules out a short-range-entangled state that preserves the non-invertible symmetry, thereby signaling the 't Hooft anomaly of a non-invertible symmetry purely in terms of unitary operators acting on a state. We then apply this framework to loop excitations in three spatial dimensions and identify a new loop excitation in the (3+1)D $\mathbb{D}_4$ topological order, which we dub a non-Abelian fermionic loop. Using the invariant of sequential circuits, we characterize the statistics of non-Abelian fermionic loops. In addition, we find a new (3+1)D mixed topological order with a single non-Abelian fermionic loop, whose long-range entanglement is protected by an invariant of sequential circuits.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates invariants defined by sequential unitary circuits that move non-invertible symmetry defects in quantum many-body systems. The Berry phase generated by moving defects defines an invariant under local deformations, which rules out short-range-entangled states preserving non-invertible symmetry. The framework is applied to identify a new non-Abelian fermionic loop excitation in (3+1)D D4 topological order and a new mixed topological order protected by sequential circuit invariants.
AI.motivation: Non-invertible symmetries (like dualities) are recently understood to play important roles in quantum many-body systems, but their physical observables and constraints are less understood. The authors aim to characterize these symmetries through unitary operators and identify new topological excitations.
AI.method: Sequential unitary circuits that move non-invertible symmetry defects; Berry phase calculations on quantum states with defects; analysis of loop excitations in 3D topological orders.
AI.result: The Berry phase from sequential circuits defines a local deformation invariant that signals 't Hooft anomaly. A new non-Abelian fermionic loop is discovered in (3+1)D D4 topological order. A new mixed topological order with single protected non-Abelian fermionic loop is also found.
AI.conclusion: Sequential circuit invariants provide a powerful tool for characterizing non-invertible symmetries and discovering new topological phases. The non-Abelian fermionic loop represents a new type of excitation in 3+1D topological orders.

title: Defect Tolerance in Trigonal Selenium Photovoltaics
summary: Understanding how point defects fundamentally influence photovoltaic performance remains a central question for emerging wide-band gap absorbers. Trigonal selenium (t-Se) has recently re-emerged as a promising photovoltaic material due to its near-optimal band gap for tandem and indoor applications. Here we quantify defect-assisted Shockley-Read-Hall (SRH) recombination in t-Se using first principles calculations across a large and chemically diverse set of point defects. Our results suggest that t-Se is intrinsically defect tolerant. Despite the presence of multiple deep levels in the gap, recombination via nonradiative multi-phonon emission processes is strongly suppressed by large lattice reorganizations and large energy releases of at least 0.5 EG per recombination event, while radiative defect-assisted capture also remains too small to account for the observed device losses. Consequently, SRH recombination mediated by realistic concentrations of point defects cannot account for the observed efficiency limitations in selenium photovoltaics. We explore trends in both radiative and nonradiative SRH recombination rates across the defect data set, highlighting their complex dependence on defect level position, lattice relaxation, charge state, and doping conditions. These findings establish trigonal selenium as a defect-tolerant wide-band-gap absorber and provide transferable design principles for optimizing next-generation photovoltaic materials for tandem and indoor applications.

AI.tldr: This study investigates point defects in trigonal selenium (t-Se) for photovoltaic applications using first-principles calculations to understand defect-assisted Shockley-Read-Hall (SRH) recombination mechanisms.
AI.motivation: Understanding how point defects fundamentally influence photovoltaic performance is crucial for emerging wide-band gap absorbers. Trigonal selenium has re-emerged as a promising photovoltaic material due to its near-optimal band gap for tandem and indoor applications.
AI.method: First principles calculations across a large and chemically diverse set of point defects to quantify defect-assisted SRH recombination in t-Se, exploring trends in both radiative and nonradiative SRH recombination rates.
AI.result: t-Se is intrinsically defect tolerant. Despite multiple deep levels in the gap, nonradiative multi-phonon emission is strongly suppressed by large lattice reorganizations and large energy releases (≥0.5 EG per recombination event). Radiative defect-assisted capture is too small to account for observed device losses. SRH recombination mediated by realistic concentrations of point defects cannot explain efficiency limitations in selenium photovoltaics.
AI.conclusion: Trigonal selenium is established as a defect-tolerant wide-band-gap absorber, providing transferable design principles for optimizing next-generation photovoltaic materials for tandem and indoor applications.

title: Structural Changes and Transport Properties of $\mathrm{YBa_2Cu_3O_7}$ Locally Modified by a He$^+$ Focused Ion Beam
summary: Irradiation of a material with ions can cause various defects that can lead to structural phase transitions and the modification of the material's properties. Here we study the irradiation of the epitaxyally grown thin films of the high-temperature superconductor $\mathrm{YBa_2Cu_3O_7}$ with $30\,\mathrm{keV}$ He$^{+}$ ions which leads to the expansion of the crystal lattice, decrease of the critical temperature $T_c$ and eventually transition to an insulator. Fabrication of such insulating regions with a focused He-Ion beam with a spot size of $\sim 10\,\mathrm{nm}$ is a powerful technique for fabrication of superconducting nano-devices. Using low-temperature resistivity measurements, diffraction with a nanofocused X-ray beam and atomic force microscopy, we investigated how the structure and the electric transport properties of $\mathrm{YBa_2Cu_3O_7}$ depend on the irradiation dose in a range $10$--$100\,\mathrm{ions/nm^2}$ and on the lateral size of the irradiated area in a range $30$--$5000\,\mathrm{nm}$.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the effects of 30 keV He+ ion irradiation on epitaxial YBa2Cu3O7 (YBCO) thin films, demonstrating that irradiation can induce a superconducting-to-insulator transition through controlled defect formation, enabling nanofabrication of superconducting devices using focused He-ion beams.
AI.motivation: Ion irradiation creates defects in materials that can alter their structural and electrical properties. For high-temperature superconductors like YBCO, understanding this irradiation-induced transition to insulation is crucial for developing nano-fabrication techniques for superconducting devices.
AI.method: The researchers irradiated epitaxially grown YBCO thin films with 30 keV He+ ions at doses ranging from 10-100 ions/nm2, varying the lateral size of irradiated areas from 30-5000 nm. They characterized the materials using low-temperature resistivity measurements, nanofocused X-ray diffraction, and atomic force microscopy.
AI.result: Irradiation causes crystal lattice expansion, decreases the critical temperature Tc, and eventually drives the material from superconducting to insulating behavior. The focused He-ion beam with ~10 nm spot size enables precise patterning of insulating regions for superconducting nano-devices.
AI.conclusion: He-ion beam irradiation is a powerful technique for fabricating superconducting nano-devices in YBCO thin films by precisely controlling the irradiation dose and lateral dimensions to achieve desired transport properties.

title: The Kondo effect in ferromagnetic quantum critical CeRh$_6$Ge$_4$
comment: 9 pages, 7 Figures, accepted in Phys. Rev. B
summary: The mechanism of a pressure-induced quantum critical point in the heavy fermion ferromagnet CeRh$_6$Ge$_4$ has attracted interest, as ferromagnetic quantum criticality in a clean itinerant Ce compound is typically avoided. The localized versus itinerant character of the 4\textit{f} electrons is a key aspect for understanding this behavior. We investigated the electronic structure of the 4\textit{f} shell in CeRh$_6$Ge$_4$ using core-level photoelectron and x-ray absorption spectroscopy, demonstrating the hybridization of Ce 4\textit{f} with the conduction electrons. Linearly polarized x-ray absorption reveals a temperature-dependent linear dichroism consistent with the crystal-electric-field (CEF) sequence as inferred from the static susceptibility. This dichroism cannot be described by an ionic full-multiplet model alone, but is reproduced by including the Kondo effect within a single-impurity Anderson model in the non crossing approximation (SIAM/NCA). The Kondo effect mixes higher lying crystal-field states into a resulting multiorbital ground state with 4\textit{f} occupancy \textit{n}$_f$\,$\sim$\,0.9. Deviations at low temperatures between the measured linear dichroism and calculated dichroism suggest an orbital-dependent Kondo effect. A scenario in which there is a multiorbital ground state and orbital-dependent Kondo hybridization should be a starting point for a model of pressure-induced criticality in CeRh$_6$Ge$_4$.

AI.tldr: Study of pressure-induced quantum critical point in heavy fermion ferromagnet CeRh6Ge4, investigating the localized vs itinerant character of 4f electrons using photoelectron and x-ray absorption spectroscopy.
AI.motivation: Understanding ferromagnetic quantum criticality in clean itinerant Ce compounds, which is typically avoided, and resolving the role of Ce 4f electron hybridization.
AI.method: Core-level photoelectron spectroscopy and x-ray absorption spectroscopy, combined with single-impurity Anderson model in non-crossing approximation (SIAM/NCA) calculations.
AI.result: Demonstrated hybridization of Ce 4f with conduction electrons; found temperature-dependent linear dichroism consistent with crystal-electric-field sequence; Kondo effect creates multi-orbital ground state with 4f occupancy ~0.9; orbital-dependent Kondo effect observed at low temperatures.
AI.conclusion: A multi-orbital ground state with orbital-dependent Kondo hybridization provides a starting model for understanding pressure-induced criticality in CeRh6Ge4.

title: Topological Phase Transition in Mechanical Honeycomb Lattice
summary: Topological materials provide a new tool to direct wave energy with unprecedented precision and robustness. Three elastic topological phases, the valley Hall, Chern and spin Hall insulators, are currently studied, and they are achieved separately in rather distinct configurations. Here, we explore analytically various topological phase transitions for in-plane elastic wave in a unified mass-spring honeycomb lattice. It is demonstrated that the three elastic topological phases can be realized in this single lattice by designing mass, stiffness or introducing Coriolis' effect. In particular, the interface between valley Hall and Chern insulators is found to support topological interface mode for the first time. Perturbation method is used to derive the analytic effective continuum model in the neighbor of band degeneracy, and the physics in topological phase transitions are revealed through evaluation of topological invariants. The topologically protected interface states, their decaying profile as well as the pseudo-spin-indicating polarization specific for elastic wave are systematically analyzed, and these results are further confirmed numerically by Bloch wave analysis of domain wall strip and transient simulation of finite sized sample. This study offers a concise and unified analytical model to explore topology nature of elastic wave, and can provide intuitive guidance to design of continuum mechanical topological materials.

AI.tldr: This paper explores three elastic topological phases (valley Hall, Chern, and spin Hall insulators) in a unified mass-spring honeycomb lattice, demonstrating they can be realized through mass/stiffness design or Coriolis effect. A novel topological interface mode is discovered at valley Hall-Chern insulator interfaces.
AI.motivation: Current topological elastic materials require separate distinct configurations for each topological phase. This work seeks a unified approach to achieve multiple topological phases in a single lattice system, enabling exploration of interface states between different topological phases.
AI.method: The authors use perturbation method to derive effective continuum models near band degeneracy, evaluate topological invariants to reveal transition physics, and validate findings through Bloch wave analysis and transient finite-size simulations.
AI.result: Three topological elastic phases are successfully realized in one unified honeycomb lattice. The valley Hall-Chern insulator interface is shown to support topological interface modes for the first time. Decaying profiles and pseudo-spin polarization characteristics of interface states are systematically analyzed.
AI.conclusion: This study provides a concise unified analytical framework for exploring elastic wave topology, offering intuitive guidance for designing continuum mechanical topological materials with protected edge/interface states.

title: Superconductivity in the pressure-amorphized topological insulator CrP$_4$
summary: The interplay among superconductivity, magnetism, and nontrivial band topology represents one of the most compelling frontiers in condensed matter physics. The exploration of novel superconductivity in 3d transition-metal compounds, particularly the rare Cr-based systems containing strongly magnetic Cr ions, has long attracted attention owing to their unconventional pairing mechanisms that challenge conventional wisdom. Yet, Cr-based superconductors remain scarce, especially those possessing nontrivial topological character, underscoring the urgent need to uncover new members. Here we report the observation of superconductivity in pressure-amorphized Cr-based topological insulator CrP$_4$. Upon compression, CrP$_4$ undergoes an anomalous quantum phase transition from a metallic to a semiconducting-like state at around 15 GPa, driven by significant changes in the electronic structure. At approximately 70 GPa, re-metallization with superconductivity occurs alongside an irreversible amorphization. The superconducting transition temperature Tc increases monotonically with pressure, reaching 4.8 K at 141.3 GPa. Furthermore, theoretical calculations predict multiple topological phase transitions from a strong topological insulator to a trivial state and finally back to a strong topological state under pressure. Our study not only establishes CrP$_4$ as the first Cr-based amorphous superconductor but also opens a new paradigm for exploring superconducting and topological properties in amorphous materials.

AI.tldr: This study reports the discovery of superconductivity in pressure-amorphized Cr-based topological insulator CrP4. Under high pressure (~70 GPa), the material undergoes re-metallization with superconductivity alongside irreversible amorphization, achieving Tc up to 4.8 K at 141.3 GPa. This represents the first Cr-based amorphous superconductor and reveals multiple topological phase transitions under pressure.
AI.motivation: Cr-based superconductors are rare, especially those with nontrivial topological character. The exploration of novel superconductivity in 3d transition-metal compounds containing strongly magnetic Cr ions has attracted attention due to their unconventional pairing mechanisms that challenge conventional wisdom. The study aims to uncover new members in this scarce family and explore superconducting and topological properties in amorphous materials.
AI.method: High-pressure experiments were conducted on CrP4, with measurements at various pressures up to 141.3 GPa. Electronic structure changes were monitored through transport measurements. Theoretical calculations were performed to predict topological phase transitions under pressure.
AI.result: CrP4 undergoes an anomalous quantum phase transition from metallic to semiconducting-like state at ~15 GPa. At ~70 GPa, re-metallization with superconductivity occurs alongside irreversible amorphization. Tc increases monotonically with pressure, reaching 4.8 K at 141.3 GPa. Theoretical calculations predict multiple topological phase transitions from a strong topological insulator to a trivial state and back to a strong topological state under pressure.
AI.conclusion: This study establishes CrP4 as the first Cr-based amorphous superconductor and opens a new paradigm for exploring superconducting and topological properties in amorphous materials. The findings highlight the potential for discovering new superconductors through pressure-induced amorphization in topological materials.

title: Phonons and magnetism of kagome FeGe probed by nuclear resonant scattering
summary: Kagome FeGe hosts a $2\times2\times2$ charge-density wave (CDW) that strongly interplays with antiferromagnetic order. Here, we report $^{57}$Fe nuclear resonant scattering measurement to study FeGe across its long-range CDW and incommensurate magnetic transitions. Upon entering the CDW state, hardening of acoustic phonons and optical phonons around 22~meV, 27~meV, and 31~meV are observed in the Fe partial phonon density of states, which can be qualitatively captured by first-principle calculations. Upon entering the incommensurate magnetic phase, neither the phonon density of states nor the hyperfine interaction parameters change significantly, although a subtle feature associated with the incommensurate magnetic order or slow fluctuations is detected in the time-domain Mössbauer spectra. These findings show that the CDW in kagome FeGe significantly modifies its lattice dynamics and magnetism, evidencing an intertwined nature of the spin, charge, and lattice degrees of freedom.

AI.tldr: This study investigates the interplay between charge-density wave (CDW) and magnetic order in Kagome FeGe using 57Fe nuclear resonant scattering. Researchers found that entering the CDW state hardens acoustic and optical phonons (22, 27, and 31 meV), while the incommensurate magnetic phase shows minimal changes in phonon DOS but subtle features in Mössbauer spectra.
AI.motivation: The motivation was to understand how the 2×2×2 CDW in Kagome FeGe interplays with antiferromagnetic order and lattice dynamics, as this represents an intertwined system of spin, charge, and lattice degrees of freedom.
AI.method: The researchers used 57Fe nuclear resonant scattering (nuclear resonant inelastic X-ray scattering and time-domain Mössbauer spectroscopy) combined with first-principles calculations to study FeGe across its CDW and incommensurate magnetic transitions.
AI.result: Upon entering the CDW state, hardening of acoustic and optical phonons around 22 meV, 27 meV, and 31 meV was observed in the Fe partial phonon density of states, which was qualitatively captured by DFT calculations. In the incommensurate magnetic phase, neither phonon DOS nor hyperfine interaction parameters changed significantly, though subtle features associated with incommensurate magnetic order or slow fluctuations were detected.
AI.conclusion: The CDW in Kagome FeGe significantly modifies lattice dynamics and magnetism, providing evidence for the intertwined nature of spin, charge, and lattice degrees of freedom in this material.

title: Screening of the Coulomb interaction in Carbon Nanotubes: A First-Principles cRPA study
summary: We investigate the electronic screening of long-range Coulomb interactions in carbon nanotubes with different chiralities using first-principles calculations within the random-phase approximation. Depending on their wrapping vector, carbon nanotubes exhibit either metallic or semiconducting behavior, providing an ideal platform to explore how reduced dimensionality and electronic structure govern screening in one-dimensional systems. The strength of on-site Coulomb interactions in these compounds falls within the range of 3.5 to 5 eV, which is approximately 2-3 eV smaller than the corresponding values in nanoribbon compounds. This reduction subsequently affects the value of long-range interactions, consistent with experimental results regarding the smaller binding energy of excitons in nanotubes. Despite their common carbon backbone, we find that the effective interaction landscape depends not only on metallicity but also sensitively on chirality and band topology. In particular, armchair and zigzag nanotubes with similar electronic character exhibit markedly different screening efficiencies. Our results establish a unified microscopic picture of electronic screening in carbon nanotubes and place them in direct context with previous first-principles studies of low-dimensional carbon nanostructures.

AI.tldr: This study investigates electronic screening of long-range Coulomb interactions in carbon nanotubes with different chiralities using first-principles calculations within the random-phase approximation. The research reveals that on-site Coulomb interactions in nanotubes (3.5-5 eV) are 2-3 eV smaller than in nanoribbons, affecting long-range interactions and exciton binding energies. Screening efficiency depends not only on metallicity but also on chirality and band topology, with armchair and zigzag nanotubes showing markedly different screening behaviors.
AI.motivation: Carbon nanotubes exhibit either metallic or semiconducting behavior depending on their wrapping vector, providing an ideal platform to explore how reduced dimensionality and electronic structure govern screening in one-dimensional systems. The study aims to understand how chirality and band topology affect electronic screening compared to other low-dimensional carbon nanostructures.
AI.method: First-principles calculations within the random-phase approximation (RPA) were used to investigate electronic screening of long-range Coulomb interactions in carbon nanotubes with different chiralities (armchair and zigzag).
AI.result: The on-site Coulomb interactions in carbon nanotubes fall within 3.5 to 5 eV, which is approximately 2-3 eV smaller than corresponding values in nanoribbon compounds. This reduction affects long-range interactions and is consistent with experimental results regarding smaller binding energy of excitons in nanotubes. The effective interaction landscape depends not only on metallicity but also sensitively on chirality and band topology. Armchair and zigzag nanotubes with similar electronic character exhibit markedly different screening efficiencies.
AI.conclusion: The results establish a unified microscopic picture of electronic screening in carbon nanotubes and place them in direct context with previous first-principles studies of low-dimensional carbon nanostructures.

title: Substrate insulated Josephson junctions for superconducting quantum circuits
summary: We have developed a fabrication technique for Josephson junctions that employs a three-dimensional patterned, low-loss substrate instead of commonly used organic resists. The technique enables the fabrication of high-quality trilayer junctions from a wide range of geometries and materials, including high-melting-point superconductors such as tantalum or niobium. The junction electrodes are free from intentionally introduced oxides and organic materials, which are known sources of decoherence. We fabricate and characterize underdamped Nb/AlOx/Nb junctions of different sizes in several geometries. Such junctions enable manufacturing of quantum circuits operating at higher speeds and elevated temperatures.

AI.tldr: A novel fabrication technique for Josephson junctions using three-dimensional patterned low-loss substrates replaces traditional organic resist methods, enabling high-quality trilayer junctions from various geometries and materials including high-melting-point superconductors like tantalum and niobium.
AI.motivation: Conventional Josephson junction fabrication uses organic resists and oxides that introduce decoherence, limiting quantum circuit performance. This work aims to eliminate these sources of decoherence by creating electrodes free from intentionally introduced oxides and organic materials.
AI.method: The technique employs a three-dimensional patterned low-loss substrate to fabricate trilayer junctions. Underdamped Nb/AlOx/Nb junctions of various sizes and geometries were fabricated and characterized to demonstrate the technique's versatility.
AI.result: The method enables fabrication of high-quality Josephson junctions from a wide range of geometries and materials, including high-melting-point superconductors. These junctions can operate at higher speeds and elevated temperatures in quantum circuits.
AI.conclusion: This substrate-based fabrication technique represents a significant advancement for quantum electronics, offering improved coherence properties and the potential for quantum circuits operating at higher speeds and temperatures than previously achievable.

title: Kinetic kagome magnetism: from self-trapping RVB polarons to semiclassical correlations
summary: To gain deeper insight into the role of hole kinetics in determining magnetism in highly frustrated doped Mott insulators, we consider the single-hole counter-Nagaoka problem on the kagome lattice, using magnetization as a tuning parameter. Near full polarization, a doped hole delocalizes upon binding reversed spins in a pattern of singlet bonds which we term resonating-valence-bond (RVB) polaron. These RVB polarons can have extremely small effective bandwidths, and hence exhibit self-trapping. By tuning the spin polarization, we track the evolution of these states toward the unpolarized sector, where we observe the emergence of $\sqrt{3}\times\sqrt{3}$ antiferromagnetic correlation reminiscent of the classical Potts and Heisenberg models on the kagome lattice. These results provide a framework to understand how RVB physics at short scales evolves into conventional magnetic correlations at long scales.

AI.tldr: This paper studies hole dynamics in doped Mott insulators on the kagome lattice, finding that near full polarization, doped holes form self-trapped resonating-valence-bond (RVB) polarons, which evolve into √3×√3 antiferromagnetic correlations at lower polarization.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand how hole kinetics determine magnetism in highly frustrated doped Mott insulators, bridging short-scale RVB physics with long-scale magnetic correlations.
AI.method: The authors use the single-hole counter-Nagaoka problem on the kagome lattice, employing magnetization as a tuning parameter to track the system's evolution from near full polarization to the unpolarized sector.
AI.result: Near full polarization, the doped hole delocalizes by binding reversed spins in singlet bond patterns, forming RVB polarons with extremely small effective bandwidths (self-trapping). Toward the unpolarized sector, √3×√3 antiferromagnetic correlations emerge, reminiscent of classical Potts and Heisenberg models on the kagome lattice.
AI.conclusion: The results provide a framework connecting RVB physics at short scales to conventional magnetic correlations at long scales in frustrated doped Mott insulators.

title: Chiral Magnons and Cycloidal Phonons in Altermagnetic CuF$_{2}$ Monolayer
summary: Altermagnetism establishes momentum-dependent spin splitting through non-symmorphic crystal symmetries, yet whether these same symmetries simultaneously govern spin and lattice collective excitations remains open. Here we show, using first-principles calculations and linear spin-wave theory, that monolayer CuF$_2$ hosts both chirality-split magnons and cycloidal phonons controlled by the same $P2_1/c$ symmetry operations. The altermagnetic order drives strongly anisotropic magnon chirality via symmetric anisotropic exchange, with Dzyaloshinskii--Moriya interactions acting as a weak secondary modulation. Crucially, the phonon and magnon chiral responses are directionally complementary: cycloidal phonon angular momentum emerges precisely where magnon chirality is symmetry-suppressed, and vice versa. The magnon bands further carry quantized Chern numbers $C^M = \pm 2$, confirming non-trivial altermagnetic topology. These results establish monolayer CuF$_2$ as a platform where a single symmetry framework engineers magnonic, phononic, and topological responses, providing a direct connection between altermagnetism and spin-lattice chirality in two-dimensional materials.

AI.tldr: Monolayer CuF2 exhibits altermagnetic properties with chirality-split magnons and cycloidal phonons governed by P2_1/c symmetry, where phonon and magnon chiral responses are directionally complementary and magnon bands carry Chern numbers ±2.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether non-symmorphic crystal symmetries that establish altermagnetic spin splitting also govern collective spin and lattice excitations, and to establish a direct connection between altermagnetism and spin-lattice chirality in 2D materials.
AI.method: First-principles calculations combined with linear spin-wave theory.
AI.result: Monolayer CuF2 hosts both chirality-split magnons and cycloidal phonons controlled by the same P2_1/c symmetry operations. The altermagnetic order drives strongly anisotropic magnon chirality via symmetric anisotropic exchange, with Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interactions as weak secondary modulation. Phonon and magnon chiral responses are directionally complementary: cycloidal phonon angular momentum emerges where magnon chirality is symmetry-suppressed, and vice versa. Magnon bands carry quantized Chern numbers C^M = ±2, confirming non-trivial altermagnetic topology.
AI.conclusion: Monolayer CuF2 serves as a platform where a single symmetry framework engineers magnonic, phononic, and topological responses, establishing a direct connection between altermagnetism and spin-lattice chirality in two-dimensional materials.

title: Structural responses incipient to pressure-driven antiferromagnetic quantum critical point of van der Waals heavy-fermion metal CeSiI
summary: CeSiI is a van der Waals heavy-fermion metal recently found to exhibit unconventional superconductivity near a pressure-induced antiferromagnetic quantum critical point (QCP) at Pc =6 GPa. Here, we report a comprehensive single-crystal X-ray diffraction study of CeSiI under high pressures up to 8.3 GPa at room temperature, revealing subtle structural responses that precede pressure-driven QCP. We find that the unit-cell volume decreases smoothly upon compression without showing any structural phase transition in the investigated pressure range. Intriguingly, we observe abrupt and concurrent anisotropic responses of the lattice parameters around Pc =6 GPa, i.e., the a-axis contracts while the c-axis enlongated suddenly, with the unit-cell volume smoothily varies with pressure. Structural refinements further show that these lattice anomalies primarily originate from changes of Ce-Ce and Ce-Si bond lengths, as well as a flattening of the inner honeycomb Si layer within the CeSiI monolayer around Pc. Our findings establish an interesting case linking pressure-driven electronic transition of QCP at low temperatures to incipient structural responses at room temperature, thereby providing fresh insight into the pressure-temperature phase diagram of CeSiI.

AI.tldr: CeSiI, a van der Waals heavy-fermion metal exhibiting unconventional superconductivity near a pressure-induced antiferromagnetic quantum critical point (QCP) at Pc = 6 GPa, shows subtle structural responses at room temperature that precede the pressure-driven QCP. Single-crystal X-ray diffraction reveals anisotropic lattice responses around Pc: a-axis contracts while c-axis elongates suddenly, with unit-cell volume varying smoothly.
AI.motivation: Investigating the structural responses that precede pressure-driven quantum critical point (QCP) in CeSiI, establishing the link between electronic transitions at low temperatures and incipient structural responses at room temperature.
AI.method: Single-crystal X-ray diffraction study under high pressures up to 8.3 GPa at room temperature, including structural refinements.
AI.result: No structural phase transition observed; abrupt anisotropic lattice responses around Pc = 6 GPa (a-axis contraction, c-axis elongation) with smooth unit-cell volume variation. Changes in Ce-Ce and Ce-Si bond lengths, and flattening of inner honeycomb Si layer within CeSiI monolayer.
AI.conclusion: Findings establish connection between pressure-driven electronic QCP transition at low temperatures and incipient structural responses at room temperature, providing fresh insight into the pressure-temperature phase diagram of CeSiI.

title: Unveiling Orbital-mediated Ultrafast Demagnetization in Rare Earth-Transition-Metal Ferrimagnets
summary: The ultimate speed limit of magnetic recording and spintronic devices is set by the efficiency of angular-momentum transfer during ultrafast demagnetization, yet its microscopic pathway in Rare-Earth-Transition-Metal (RE-TM) ferrimagnets remains debated. Here, we establish an orbital-mediated framework in which 3d spin-orbit coupling (SOC) governs angular momentum (AM) dissipation. Strong 3d-SOC in RE-Co enables sub-picosecond, single-step demagnetization via direct orbital-to-lattice transfer, whereas weak 3d-SOC in RE-Fe redirects AM into 4f orbitals, producing slower two-step dynamics. The second-stage rate scales with 4f-SOC strength, revealing a distinct orbital-mediated dissipation channel. Using time-resolved magneto-optical Kerr measurements, supported by an extended four-temperature model, corroborate this picture across diverse RE-TM systems (RE = Sm, Gd, Tb, Dy, Ho and TM = Fe, Co, CoNi). Our results identify the SOC-driven competition between 3d and 4f orbital channels as the universal mechanism governing ultrafast demagnetization in RE-TM ferrimagnets, enabling rational design of the switching speed for next-generation spintronic devices.

AI.tldr: This paper reveals the microscopic mechanism governing ultrafast demagnetization in Rare-Earth-Transition-Metal (RE-TM) ferrimagnets. The key finding is that 3d spin-orbit coupling (SOC) determines whether demagnetization occurs via a fast single-step process (direct orbital-to-lattice transfer) or a slower two-step process (redirecting AM into 4f orbitals). This SOC-driven competition between 3d and 4f orbital channels establishes a universal mechanism for ultrafast demagnetization, enabling rational design of next-generation spintronic devices.
AI.motivation: The ultimate speed limit of magnetic recording and spintronic devices is constrained by the efficiency of angular-momentum transfer during ultrafast demagnetization. However, the microscopic pathway of this process in RE-TM ferrimagnets remained unclear, hindering the rational design of faster magnetic devices.
AI.method: Time-resolved magneto-optical Kerr effect measurements were conducted across diverse RE-TM systems (RE = Sm, Gd, Tb, Dy, Ho; TM = Fe, Co, CoNi). The experimental data were analyzed using an extended four-temperature model to quantify the demagnetization dynamics.
AI.result: Strong 3d-SOC in RE-Co enables sub-picosecond, single-step demagnetization through direct orbital-to-lattice transfer. In contrast, weak 3d-SOC in RE-Fe redirects angular momentum into 4f orbitals, producing slower two-step dynamics where the second-stage rate scales with 4f-SOC strength. This reveals a distinct orbital-mediated dissipation channel.
AI.conclusion: The SOC-driven competition between 3d and 4f orbital channels is identified as the universal mechanism governing ultrafast demagnetization in RE-TM ferrimagnets. This framework enables rational design of switching speed for next-generation spintronic devices by selecting appropriate RE-TM combinations with desired SOC characteristics.

title: Observation of coherently modulated phonon band and lifetime in superlattice
summary: Similar to the behavior of elementary particles, such as photons and electrons, the interference of phonon waves in artificial periodic nanostructures coherently modulates phonon band structures, serving as the foundation for phonon band engineering. However, direct observation of such coherently modulated phonon band structures remains challenging despite substantial insights from existing literature. Here, utilizing high-resolution inelastic X-ray scattering, we observed coherently modulated phonon band structures with phononic band gaps in a short-period GaAs/AlAs superlattice at 300 K and 500 K. Our findings provide the first direct evidence of phonon coherence at and above room temperatures, signifying a major advancement in the artificial engineering of phonon band structures. Furthermore, our experimental observations and ab initio lattice dynamics revealed that the coherently modulated phonon band structure enhances three-phonon scattering channels, strengthening high-order anharmonic effects such as three-phonon scattering and optical phonon softening. Our observations demonstrate the robustness of phonon coherence at high temperatures, and opens new routes for engineering phonon band structure and high-order phonon-phonon scattering by employing a flexible, bottom-up nanostructuring approach, with extensive applications in phononic metamaterials, microelectronics, and thermoelectrics.

AI.tldr: This study reports the first direct observation of coherently modulated phonon band structures with phononic band gaps in short-period GaAs/AlAs superlattices at 300 K and 500 K using high-resolution inelastic X-ray scattering, demonstrating phonon coherence above room temperatures.
AI.motivation: Direct observation of coherently modulated phonon band structures in artificial periodic nanostructures has remained challenging despite its importance for phonon band engineering. The field lacked experimental evidence of phonon coherence at and above room temperature, limiting the development of phononic metamaterials and related applications.
AI.method: High-resolution inelastic X-ray scattering (IXS) was employed to directly observe phonon band structures in short-period GaAs/AlAs superlattices. Measurements were conducted at 300 K and 500 K. Ab initio lattice dynamics calculations were also performed to support experimental observations.
AI.result: The study provides the first direct evidence of phonon coherence at and above room temperatures (300 K and 500 K). Coherently modulated phonon band structures with phononic band gaps were observed in the short-period GaAs/AlAs superlattice. The results also reveal that the coherently modulated band structure enhances three-phonon scattering channels, strengthening high-order anharmonic effects such as three-phonon scattering and optical phonon softening.
AI.conclusion: This research demonstrates the robustness of phonon coherence at high temperatures and opens new routes for engineering phonon band structures and high-order phonon-phonon scattering through flexible bottom-up nanostructuring approaches, with extensive applications in phononic metamaterials, microelectronics, and thermoelectrics.

title: Integrated magnonic neural circuits based on nonlinear wave neurons
summary: Artificial intelligence is driving intense interest in alternative computing hardware capable of neural information processing beyond conventional charge-based electronics. Among emerging approaches, wave-based computing promises highly parallel and energy-efficient operation, but scalable physical neural hardware has remained elusive because wave systems generally lack cascadable nonlinear neurons with signal regeneration and phase-robust operation. Here we demonstrate integrated magnonic neural circuits based on nonlinear threshold neurons realized in nanoscale yttrium iron garnet waveguides. The neurons perform weighted summation of multiple spin-wave inputs, while a pump-controlled nonlinear activation defines continuously tunable firing thresholds. Owing to deeply nonlinear spin-wave dynamics, the activated neurons emit self-normalized outputs whose intensities are largely independent of the input amplitudes, while nonlinear phase self-adjustment suppresses sensitivity to the relative input phases, enabling deterministic neuron-to-neuron cascading without external signal restoration. We experimentally realize programmable threshold neurons, reconfigurable weighted classification and deterministic cascading between sequential neuronal stages, and further demonstrate reconfigurable physical pattern recognition in a seven-neuron integrated magnonic circuit through experimental classification of the binary letter patterns 'HUST'. These results establish nonlinear magnons as a scalable platform for integrated neural hardware and position nonlinear wave dynamics as a general paradigm for physical neuromorphic computing.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates integrated magnonic neural circuits using nonlinear threshold neurons in nanoscale yttrium iron garnet waveguides for wave-based computing, enabling scalable physical neuromorphic computing with deterministic neuron-to-neuron cascading.
AI.motivation: AI is driving intense interest in alternative computing hardware for neural information processing beyond conventional electronics. Wave-based computing promises highly parallel and energy-efficient operation, but scalable physical neural hardware has remained elusive due to lack of cascadable nonlinear neurons with signal regeneration and phase-robust operation.
AI.method: The researchers realized nonlinear threshold neurons in nanoscale yttrium iron garnet waveguides. These neurons perform weighted summation of multiple spin-wave inputs, with a pump-controlled nonlinear activation defining continuously tunable firing thresholds. The deeply nonlinear spin-wave dynamics enable self-normalized outputs and nonlinear phase self-adjustment.
AI.result: They experimentally demonstrated programmable threshold neurons, reconfigurable weighted classification, deterministic cascading between sequential neuronal stages, and physical pattern recognition of binary letter patterns 'HUST' in a seven-neuron integrated magnonic circuit.
AI.conclusion: Nonlinear magnons represent a scalable platform for integrated neural hardware, establishing nonlinear wave dynamics as a general paradigm for physical neuromorphic computing.

title: Bulk-like Compressibility of the Au-Au Metallic Bond in the Atomically Precise $\mathrm{Au}_{25}$ Cluster
comment: Draft: 13 pages, 4 figures. Supporting information: 48 pages
summary: We present a high-pressure single-crystal X-ray diffraction study of the atomically precise $\mathrm{Au}_{25}(\mathrm{PET})_{18}^{q}$ cluster ($q=-1,0$) up to 10 GPa under strictly hydrostatic conditions. Our crystallographic analysis provides direct evidence for the pressure-induced phase transitions previously suggested by spectroscopic studies. Structural refinements reveal that the cluster accommodates compression through the reorganization of the flexible ligand shell and secondary distortions of the staple motifs, while the $\mathrm{Au}_{13}$ icosahedral core remains intact. Notably, the internal Au-Au distances exhibit a monotonic contraction that quantitatively mirrors the compressibility of bulk gold. This invariant rigidity at the sub-nanometer scale demonstrates that the fundamental stiffness of the metallic bond is preserved regardless of size. Our findings reconcile previous contradictions in the elasticity of metal nanostructures by isolating the intrinsic mechanical response of the gold kernel from extrinsic structural and experimental artifacts.

AI.tldr: High-pressure single-crystal X-ray diffraction study of Au25(PET)18 clusters reveals pressure-induced phase transitions, with the ligand shell reorganizing to accommodate compression while the Au13 icosahedral core remains intact and exhibits bulk-like compressibility.
AI.motivation: To reconcile previous contradictions in the elasticity of metal nanostructures by isolating the intrinsic mechanical response of the gold kernel from extrinsic structural artifacts, and to provide direct evidence for pressure-induced phase transitions previously suggested by spectroscopic studies.
AI.method: High-pressure single-crystal X-ray diffraction study under strictly hydrostatic conditions up to 10 GPa, investigating atomically precise Au25(PET)18q clusters (q=-1, 0).
AI.result: The cluster accommodates compression through reorganization of the flexible ligand shell and secondary distortions of staple motifs, while the Au13 icosahedral core remains intact. Internal Au-Au distances show monotonic contraction quantitatively mirroring bulk gold compressibility.
AI.conclusion: The invariant rigidity at the sub-nanometer scale demonstrates that fundamental metallic bond stiffness is preserved regardless of size, isolating the intrinsic mechanical response from experimental artifacts.

title: Modelling magnetic material properties with uncertainty-aware neural networks
summary: Machine learning is increasingly applied to accelerate the discovery of novel materials by exploring large compositional and structural design spaces. Yet, the scarcity of high-quality data and the frequent need for out-of-distribution prediction introduce substantial uncertainty, making the assessment of model reliability essential. In this work, we investigate uncertainty quantification as a means to evaluate model confidence in the context of permanent magnet research. In a first study, we benchmark classical and modern machine learning models for predicting intrinsic magnetic properties, focusing on the quality of their uncertainty estimates. We apply Gaussian negative log-likelihood loss and dropout-based Bayesian approximation as practical strategies for estimating predictive uncertainty. In a second study, we transfer these architectural features for uncertainty estimation to a more complex task: predicting coercivity from microstructural information using a graph neural network. Together, these studies demonstrate that uncertainty quantification not only enhances the trustworthiness of predictions but is also transferable across different modeling tasks.

AI.tldr: This work applies machine learning with uncertainty quantification to accelerate permanent magnet discovery, addressing data scarcity and out-of-distribution prediction challenges.
AI.motivation: Machine learning enables exploration of large design spaces for novel materials, but predictions are uncertain due to limited data and out-of-distribution samples—making reliability assessment critical.
AI.method: Two studies were conducted: (1) Benchmarking classical and modern ML models for intrinsic magnetic properties using Gaussian negative log-likelihood loss and dropout-based Bayesian approximation for uncertainty estimation; (2) Transferring the uncertainty estimation approach to a graph neural network for predicting coercivity from microstructural information.
AI.result: Uncertainty quantification enhances prediction trustworthiness and proves transferable across different modeling tasks in permanent magnet research.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty quantification is essential for reliable ML-driven materials discovery and can be effectively transferred across related prediction tasks.

title: Sonochemically Boosted Hydrogen Evolution Activity of Janus TMD Monolayers
summary: 2D electrocatalysts that enable hydrogen evolution at low overpotentials offer an attractive alternative to expensive platinum-based systems. Here, we report the growth of Janus transition metal dichalcogenide (TMD) monolayers (MLs), SeMoS and SeWS, on Au foils using chemical vapor deposition, and systematically compare their catalytic properties in the context of hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) with those of their parent TMDs. The Janus MLs exhibited significantly enhanced catalytic performance relative to the parent TMDs. Furthermore, these MLs on Au foils were subjected to sonochemical treatment in polar and non-polar solvents, in which the treatment with polar solvents led to a substantial improvement in the HER activity of Janus MLs. In particular, SeMoS MLs treated with water showed a low overpotential of ~63 mV, a Tafel slope of ~42 mV/dec, and an exchange current density of ~10$^{-3}$ mA cm$^{- 2}$, approaching that of platinum. Analyses indicate that enhanced electrocatalytic activity is associated with tensile strain induced by Au surface restructuring and the formation of defects in Janus MLs, as shown by experimental observations and by density functional theory calculations. The enhancement in catalytic performance due to sonochemical treatment emphasizes the importance of our results for developing novel catalytic systems for HER based on Janus 2D materials.

AI.tldr: This study develops Janus transition metal dichalcogenide (TMD) monolayers (SeMoS and SeWS) as efficient 2D electrocatalysts for hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), achieving platinum-like performance through chemical vapor deposition on Au foils followed by sonochemical treatment in polar solvents.
AI.motivation: Platinum-based HER catalysts are expensive, creating need for cheaper alternatives that can achieve low overpotentials.
AI.method: Growth of Janus TMD monolayers (SeMoS, SeWS) on Au foils using chemical vapor deposition; sonochemical treatment in polar and non-polar solvents; characterization using experimental observations and density functional theory calculations.
AI.result: Janus MLs showed significantly enhanced HER activity compared to parent TMDs. SeMoS treated with water achieved low overpotential ~63 mV, Tafel slope ~42 mV/dec, and exchange current density ~10⁻³ mA cm⁻², approaching platinum. Enhanced activity attributed to tensile strain from Au surface restructuring and defect formation.
AI.conclusion: Sonochemical treatment in polar solvents and tensile strain-induced effects make Janus 2D materials promising for developing novel HER catalytic systems.

title: Spin-Polarized Electronic Structure and Chemical Bonding Data for 2,500+ Halide Double Perovskites
summary: Halide double perovskites (A$_2$BB'X$_6$) are a long-known class of materials that has recently been rediscovered for diverse applications, including photovoltaics, photocatalysis, and radiation detection. Their doubled unit cell provides immense chemical tunability, allowing the incorporation of magnetic ions and enabling access to a wide range of electronic-structure features, including different band-edge characters, alignments, and symmetries. Magnetic elements may further introduce spin degrees of freedom and magnetic behaviour, thereby broadening the functional landscape of these compounds. Here, we present the first comprehensive database of spin-polarised electronic-structure data for all halide double perovskites predicted to be stable by the recently introduced $τ$ tolerance factor by Bartel et al. The dataset focuses on the Cs$_2$BB'X$_6$ family, with X = I, Br, Cl, and F, and includes density of states (DOS) for $>$2,500 compounds, calculated using hybrid-functional density functional theory. Among these, 719 compounds exhibit band gaps in the visible range and 118 display half-metallic character. In addition, we provide chemical-bonding analysis using \textsc{lobster}, which provides insights into orbital interactions across the dataset. To facilitate exploration, we further offer UMAP-based visualisations and an interactive app for systematic investigation of chemical composition, electronic structure, and magnetic properties.

AI.tldr: This paper presents the first comprehensive database of spin-polarized electronic structure data for halide double perovskites (Cs2BB'X6), including >2,500 compounds calculated using hybrid DFT, with 719 showing visible band gaps and 118 displaying half-metallic character.
AI.motivation: Halide double perovskites have been rediscovered for applications in photovoltaics, photocatalysis, and radiation detection, but there was no comprehensive database of their spin-polarized electronic properties. The doubled unit cell provides immense chemical tunability, allowing incorporation of magnetic ions and access to various electronic structure features.
AI.method: The authors used the τ tolerance factor by Bartel et al. to predict stable compounds, then calculated density of states (DOS) for >2,500 compounds using hybrid-functional density functional theory. They performed chemical bonding analysis using LOBSTER and offered UMAP-based visualizations and an interactive app.
AI.result: Among the dataset, 719 compounds exhibit band gaps in the visible range and 118 display half-metallic character. The dataset covers the Cs2BB'X6 family with X = I, Br, Cl, and F.
AI.conclusion: This comprehensive database provides systematic investigation tools for chemical composition, electronic structure, and magnetic properties of halide double perovskites, facilitating exploration for diverse applications in energy and technology fields.

title: Boron Co-Alloying in AlScN Wurtzite Ferroelectrics: Insights from an 850-Sample Combinatorial Study
summary: AlScN wurtzite ferroelectrics are promising candidates for energy-efficient non-volatile memory. However, AlScN suffers from a high coercive field and reduced cycling endurance, and the limited tunability of its properties constrains further optimization. Co-doping AlScN with boron offers the promise of independently tailoring the chemical and structural properties, making AlScBN an attractive quaternary system. This material has already been explored for a few selected compositions, however, no systematic study of the full AlScBN compositional space exists. A combinatorial approach consisting of gradient deposition with HiPIMS at low temperatures of 250°C and automatic analysis of film properties allowed us to analyze a total of 850 unique samples within the AlScBN phase space. In addition to a full screening of the materials' chemical and structural properties, we fabricate and characterize combinatorial device libraries. XPS charge transfer analysis experimentally confirms that bond ionicity correlates with a reduction in the coercive field for AlScN and AlScBN systems, opposite trends are instead observed for AlBN. While the films maintain a high remanent polarization of 130-150 μC/cm2, Sc and B co-doping reduces the coercive field from 7 MV/cm to 3 MV/cm. Notably, B co-alloying lowers the amount of Sc needed to lower the coercive field, reducing reliance on this scarce element. In addition, we find that co-alloying with B, notably improves cycling endurance, which is related to a reduction in defect density. These results establish AlScBN as a scalable, CMOS-compatible ferroelectric, positioning it as an interesting alternative to AlScN.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates AlScBN as a CMOS-compatible quaternary ferroelectric system for energy-efficient non-volatile memory applications. Using combinatorial deposition with HiPIMS, 850 samples were analyzed to systematically explore the AlScBN compositional space.
AI.motivation: AlScN ferroelectrics suffer from high coercive fields and limited cycling endurance, constraining their practical applications. The researchers aimed to address these limitations through boron co-doping to independently tailor chemical and structural properties.
AI.method: Combinatorial approach using gradient deposition with HiPIMS at low temperatures (250°C) and automatic analysis of film properties. This enabled screening of 850 unique samples across the AlScBN phase space, including both materials' properties and device characterization.
AI.result: XPS charge transfer analysis confirmed bond ionicity correlates with reduced coercive field. Films maintained high remanent polarization (130-150 μC/cm²). Sc and B co-doping reduced coercive field from 7 MV/cm to 3 MV/cm. B co-alloying lowered the amount of Sc needed and improved cycling endurance by reducing defect density.
AI.conclusion: AlScBN establishes as a scalable, CMOS-compatible ferroelectric and promising alternative to AlScN, offering reduced coercive field, decreased Sc reliance, and improved endurance.

title: Interfacial Coupling and Sparse Intercalation of 7-Atom-Wide Armchair Graphene Nanoribbons by N-Heterocyclic Carbene Monolayers
summary: Graphene nanoribbons (GNRs) synthesized on metal substrates experience electronic coupling and screening from the underlying surface, which, although often weak, can modify their observed properties and complicate their transfer to device-compatible substrates. Intercalation of GNRs by self-assembled monolayers (SAMs) offers a possible route to reduce this interaction. Here, we investigate the intercalation of 7-atom-wide armchair graphene nanoribbons (7-AGNRs) on Au(111) using N-heterocyclic carbenes (NHCs). Low-temperature scanning tunneling microscopy and spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, and density functional theory calculations reveal that the adsorption geometry of the NHCs strongly influences the intercalation yield for GNRs. Methyl-substituted NHCs form flat-lying dimers that partially intercalate the GNRs, producing locally decoupled segments. In contrast, bulkier isopropyl-substituted NHCs form upright monomers that embed the GNRs within the monolayer, preventing intercalation. The low intercalation yield indicates that lifting the nanoribbon from the Au surface is energetically costly. These results establish molecular adsorption geometry and packing as key parameters controlling intercalation at GNR-metal interfaces, with implications for the rational design of decoupling layers for GNR-based device integration pathways.

AI.tldr: This study explores using N-heterocyclic carbenes (NHCs) to intercalate 7-armchair graphene nanoribbons (7-AGNRs) on Au(111) surfaces, aiming to reduce electronic coupling between GNRs and the metal substrate. Using STM, Raman spectroscopy, and DFT calculations, researchers found that NHC substituent size critically determines intercalation success: smaller methyl groups allow partial intercalation via flat-lying dimers, while larger isopropyl groups prevent intercalation by forming upright monomers that embed GNRs in the monolayer.
AI.motivation: Graphene nanoribbons synthesized on metal substrates experience unwanted electronic coupling and screening from the underlying surface, which modifies their properties and complicates transfer to device-compatible substrates. Finding ways to decouple GNRs from metal surfaces is essential for integrating these materials into practical electronic devices.
AI.method: The researchers combined low-temperature scanning tunneling microscopy and spectroscopy (STM/STS), Raman spectroscopy, and density functional theory (DFT) calculations to investigate NHC intercalation of 7-AGNRs on Au(111). They tested two NHC variants with different substituent sizes: methyl and isopropyl groups.
AI.result: Molecular adsorption geometry strongly influences intercalation yield. Methyl-substituted NHCs form flat-lying dimers that partially intercalate GNRs, creating locally decoupled segments. In contrast, bulkier isopropyl-substituted NHCs form upright monomers that embed GNRs within the monolayer, preventing intercalation. Overall, lifting GNRs from the Au surface remains energetically costly, resulting in low intercalation yields.
AI.conclusion: Molecular adsorption geometry and packing are key parameters controlling intercalation at GNR-metal interfaces. This insight provides a rational design principle for developing decoupling layers in GNR-based device integration pathways. The work highlights the importance of considering molecular sterics when engineering buffer layers for printable electronics.

title: Coupling Chirality, Polar Order, and Altermagnetic Spin Splitting in a Hybrid Manganese Chloride
summary: Hybrid manganese halides enable the coexistence of molecular chirality, polar order, and magnetic exchange within a single lattice. Here, we combine first-principles calculations with spin-space-group analysis to investigate the synthesized enantiomeric pair [(R)/(S)-MPA]2[MnCl4(H2O)] (MPA = beta-methylphenethylammonium). We predict that its compensated magnetic state hosts altermagnetic spin splitting in the nonrelativistic limit, and that the coupled chiral, polar, and magnetic degrees of freedom define a symmetry-related manifold.
